name: Convoy
summary: Seattle-based digital freight network that used machine-learning pricing and automated matching to connect shippers with truckers, backed by Bezos Expeditions, Baillie Gifford and T. Rowe Price at a $3.8B peak valuation. Ceased operations in October 2023 after failing to find a buyer; Flexport later acquired its technology assets for about $16M.
lesson: Algorithmic freight-matching still runs on thin trucking margins; a capital-markets pullback plus a freight recession can sink even a $1B+-funded network almost overnight.

name: Embark Trucks
summary: Developer of self-driving software for long-haul Class 8 trucks that went public via SPAC in 2021. Never generated meaningful revenue; laid off 70% of staff and wound down operations in March 2023 as capital markets turned away from pre-revenue autonomy plays.
lesson: A SPAC's headline valuation is not commercial traction — autonomous trucking needs years of regulatory and technical runway that public-market patience for pre-revenue names does not provide.

name: Starsky Robotics
summary: Early autonomous trucking startup that combined highway self-driving with remote teleoperation for complex maneuvers, and ran the first fully unmanned truck on a live U.S. highway in 2019. Shut down in March 2020 after failing to raise a follow-on round.
lesson: Investors preferred higher-margin, capital-light bets over autonomous trucking's large capex and slow regulatory path, even when the underlying technology worked.

name: TuSimple
summary: Autonomous trucking company that IPO'd on Nasdaq in 2021 at an $8.5B valuation. Founders were accused of covertly diverting IP and driverless-truck technology to an undisclosed China-based venture; the company settled a shareholder fraud lawsuit for $189M, was delisted from Nasdaq, and wound down its U.S. operations, laying off 75% of its American workforce.
lesson: Governance failures and undisclosed conflicts of interest by founders can destroy an autonomous-vehicle company faster than any technical setback, wiping out a multibillion-dollar public listing.

name: Bossa Nova Robotics
summary: Maker of AI-powered shelf-scanning robots for retail inventory tracking, deployed in hundreds of Walmart stores. Walmart abruptly ended its contract in November 2020, deciding manual processes were cheaper and simpler; Bossa Nova laid off half its staff and wound down its core retail robotics business.
lesson: Being dependent on a single anchor retail customer for a physical-robotics deployment is an existential risk — the customer can revert to cheaper manual processes overnight.

name: Berkshire Grey
summary: AI-powered warehouse robotics and piece-picking automation company that went public via SPAC in 2021 at a $2.2B valuation. Stock traded below $3 for over a year amid persistent losses; SoftBank took the company private in 2023 for about $375M, a fraction of its de-SPAC valuation.
lesson: SPAC-driven valuations for capital-intensive robotics businesses can collapse quickly when profitability targets slip — going public does not fix an underlying cash-burn problem.
source: Seeking Alpha, Bloomberg Law, SEC filings (2023)

name: Attabotics
summary: Calgary robotics company that built a cube-based, ant-colony-inspired goods-to-person warehouse storage system meant to eliminate aisles. Filed for bankruptcy protection in June 2025 after a relentless cash burn, warehouse fires, and the insolvency of major client Nordstrom; a lawsuit against Canadian Tire further strained key retail relationships.
lesson: Novel warehouse hardware architectures carry real physical failure risk (fires, reliability issues), and litigating against anchor customers instead of resolving disputes collaboratively can accelerate collapse.

name: Fabric (CommonSense Robotics)
summary: Israeli micro-fulfillment robotics company (rebranded from CommonSense Robotics) that automated small urban warehouses for rapid grocery delivery. Cut 40% of staff in 2022 while pivoting from an operated-network model to a software/hardware platform sale, then cut another 15% in 2024 as the rapid-delivery thesis cooled.
lesson: Building and operating your own physical fulfillment network is far more capital-intensive than licensing the software — a mid-flight business-model pivot signals the original model's unit economics never worked.

name: Noodle.ai (Daybreak)
summary: San Francisco enterprise AI company selling 'FlowOps' supply-chain optimization software to manufacturers. Underwent four rounds of layoffs in three years amid chronic unprofitability and organizational churn, and ultimately rebranded as Daybreak.
lesson: Repeated 'unplanned' layoff cycles and a rebrand under distress are hallmark signs that enterprise AI sales cycles were too long and expensive relative to contract value.
source: Glassdoor, diginomica, Built In San Francisco (2021-2024)

name: Transfix
summary: Digital freight brokerage using machine-learning to match shippers and carriers; attempted a SPAC merger in 2021 that was derailed by market turmoil, then took a $40M rescue round the same day Convoy shut down. Sold its brokerage business to NFI in mid-2024, retaining just over 100 of roughly 350 employees to sell its software standalone.
lesson: Being 'the last one standing' in a freight recession is not a strategy — selling the money-losing brokerage arm to preserve the software layer is often a face-saving wind-down, not a turnaround.

name: IAM Robotics (Onward Robotics)
summary: Pittsburgh warehouse-picking robotics company that laid off a significant share of staff in January 2023 while launching a new goods-to-person robot, having already pivoted away from its original mobile-manipulation platform in 2021. Rebranded as Onward Robotics later in 2023.
lesson: Two forced product pivots and a rebrand within two years is a pattern consistent with a robotics company that never found a reliably sellable warehouse-automation product.

name: 6 River Systems
summary: Maker of the 'Chuck' collaborative autonomous mobile robot for warehouse picking, acquired by Shopify for $450M in 2019 as part of its logistics push. Shopify exited logistics in 2023, selling 6 River Systems to Ocado for just $12.7M — roughly 3% of the original purchase price.
lesson: A robotics acquisition can be technically sound while still destroying enormous value if the acquirer's broader strategic bet (Shopify's vertical logistics push) fails and forces a fire sale.

name: Ike (Ike Robotics)
summary: Autonomous highway trucking startup founded by ex-Google/Uber and Otto engineers. Acquired by Nuro in 2022 primarily for its engineering team and technology, with the independent Ike product and brand discontinued shortly after.
lesson: An acquihire framed as a 'partnership' is frequently the softest available landing for an autonomous-vehicle startup that could not independently fund the years of testing needed to commercialize.

name: Nuro
summary: Autonomous delivery-robot company (R2 vehicle) that raised over $2B including a landmark SoftBank round, then cut roughly 30% of staff in 2022 and again scaled back operations in 2024, pivoting from operating its own delivery fleet to licensing its self-driving stack to automakers and mobility companies.
lesson: Even the best-funded autonomy startups can be forced into a licensing pivot when the capital and time required to operate a proprietary vehicle fleet at scale outlast investor patience.

name: Rethink Robotics
summary: Pioneer of collaborative robots (Baxter and Sawyer) for manufacturing and light industrial tasks, founded by Rodney Brooks. Closed after a decade when sales fell far short of targets and mainstream industrial-automation incumbents (ABB, FANUC, Universal Robots) out-executed it commercially; assets were sold to Germany's HAHN Group.
lesson: Being first to market with a genuinely novel robot is not sufficient if incumbent industrial-automation players can match the capability and out-sell you through existing enterprise channels.
source: IEEE Spectrum, ASME, The Robot Report (2018)

name: Dispatch (delivery robots)
summary: Sidewalk delivery-robot startup acquired by Amazon in 2019, whose technology became the basis for the Amazon Scout delivery robot program. Amazon cancelled Scout in January 2023, effectively ending Dispatch's product line entirely.
lesson: Being acquired by a tech giant is not a guaranteed survival path — internal strategic priorities at the acquirer can shut down even a technically functioning delivery-robot program years later.

name: Peloton Technology
summary: Developer of truck 'platooning' technology that let pairs of trucks drive at close following distances using vehicle-to-vehicle communication and automated braking to improve fuel efficiency and safety. Repeatedly missed its own commercialization deadlines and ceased operations, entering liquidation in summer 2021.
lesson: Platooning promised clear fuel-savings ROI but repeatedly slipped commercialization timelines against regulatory and fleet-adoption friction until capital ran out.

name: Locomation
summary: Carnegie Mellon-spinout autonomous trucking startup building driver-guided automated truck convoys ('Autonomous Relay Convoy'). Reduced most non-engineering staff citing lack of funds in February 2023, initially denied it was shutting down, then closed later that year.
lesson: In a capital-starved environment for autonomous trucking, public denials of a shutdown followed swiftly by closure erode both employee and investor trust — transparency earlier would have preserved more optionality.

name: Slync.io
summary: Goldman Sachs-backed logistics orchestration software startup automating freight workflows with AI. Collapsed after its ousted founder and CEO, Chris Kirchner, was indicted and later convicted of wire fraud and money laundering for diverting $25M of investor funds for personal use; the company went into liquidation in October 2023 despite a $24M rescue infusion.
lesson: Founder financial fraud can destroy a well-funded, technically credible logistics-AI company even after new management and emergency capital try to save it.
source: FreightWaves, Journal of Commerce, Wikipedia (Chris Kirchner) (2023-2024)

name: Deliv
summary: Crowdsourced same-day delivery and route-optimization platform serving retailers like Best Buy, Macy's and Walgreens. Shut down in mid-2020 after failing to reach profitability amid COVID-driven demand spikes that strained its finances rather than saving them; Target acquired its routing/batching technology.
lesson: A surge in demand does not automatically fix broken unit economics — Deliv's costs scaled with volume as fast as revenue, and investors declined to fund the gap.
source: Supply Chain Dive, CNBC, Retail Dive (2020)

name: Osaro
summary: AI-powered robotic picking, kitting and depalletizing company for warehouse automation. Relied on late-stage debt financing ($17.6M debt round in December 2023) alongside equity to keep operating, a pattern typical of capital-constrained robotics companies stretching runway.
lesson: Turning to venture debt after a decade of equity raises without a clear profitability path is a warning sign that the underlying picking-robot unit economics have not scaled.
source: The Robot Report, CB Insights, Tracxn (2023)

name: Doddle
summary: London-based reverse-logistics and parcel-collection startup that burned through tens of millions of pounds (losing £24M on just £687K revenue in 2015) operating standalone high-street stores, closing 17 stores and laying off over 100 staff in 2017. Pivoted to a pure software/kiosk model for returns and last-mile logistics, and was ultimately acquired by Blue Yonder in November 2023.
lesson: A capital-intensive physical retail footprint built to solve a software problem (parcel returns) was the wrong architecture from the start — the company only became viable after abandoning stores entirely for an IT-provider model.

name: Celect
summary: Boston-based AI demand-sensing and predictive analytics company acquired by Nike in 2019 to power real-time inventory forecasting, cutting prediction time from six months to 30 minutes. Its 30-minute demand model could not account for compounding shocks — a freight crisis, factory shutdowns and simultaneous overproduction across styles — contributing to Nike's high-profile 2022 inventory glut, after which the platform quietly disappeared from Nike's public technology narrative.
lesson: A forecasting model tuned for normal-condition demand patterns can fail badly under simultaneous, compounding supply shocks — replacing a slower but shock-tested legacy process entirely, rather than running in parallel, magnified the damage.
source: CNBC, Crunchbase News, industry supply-chain retrospectives (2019-2022)

name: Bright Machines
summary: AI-powered 'software-defined microfactory' company combining computer vision and machine learning for automated electronics assembly and inspection, backed by BlackRock in a $106M Series C. Closed its Israel R&D center and cut staff amid internal reports of shifting strategy and leadership churn.
lesson: Ambitious 'AI microfactory' automation platforms require enormous integration engineering per customer site; repeated strategic resets and R&D center closures signal the company could not standardize its offering fast enough to reach efficient scale.

name: Sennder
summary: Berlin-based digital freight forwarder using AI-driven pricing and matching algorithms to run a 'digital fleet' of trucking capacity across Europe, once a European trucking unicorn. Conducted significant layoffs in January 2023 amid a broad European freight-forwarding downturn and unprecedented rate corrections.
lesson: A pan-European 'digital fleet' model built on thin trucking margins was highly exposed to the 2022-2023 freight rate collapse, forcing deep workforce cuts even at a well-funded unicorn.

name: Cargomatic
summary: 'Uber for truckers' local drayage and trucking marketplace that burned through roughly $15M in investor money and bank loans while deliberately operating at a loss. Lost its CEO, CFO and COO in quick succession, laid off roughly half its staff across two rounds, and became dependent on a single customer to survive.
lesson: A 'tech platform' that actually depends on manual data entry by staff was never the capital-light marketplace investors were pricing in — customer concentration risk and leadership churn compounded the underlying cash burn.

name: Deliverr
summary: AI-driven fast-fulfillment and warehouse-network logistics startup acquired by Shopify for $2.1B in 2022 as the centerpiece of Shopify's push to compete with Amazon on delivery speed. Shopify abandoned the fulfillment strategy within a year, taking a $1.6B impairment on Deliverr and 6 River Systems before selling the combined logistics business to Flexport for an equity stake in 2023.
lesson: A blockbuster acquisition price is not validation of the underlying business — Deliverr's fulfillment network could not make Shopify's vertical logistics bet profitable fast enough, resulting in a $1.6B write-down within 18 months.
source: Wikipedia (Deliverr), Supply Chain Dive, RMW Commerce Consulting (2022-2023)

name: Freightos
summary: Israeli freight-booking marketplace using AI-driven rate comparison across ocean, air and land freight, which IPO'd on Nasdaq in January 2023. Stock fell over 30% after founder Zvi Schreiber stepped down from the board, the company has never turned a profit, and it announced cutting up to 15% of its workforce in 2025-2026 to reach adjusted profitability.
lesson: Going public does not solve a marketplace's fundamental unprofitability — years after IPO, Freightos was still cutting headcount to reach breakeven, with founder departure adding further uncertainty for public investors.
source: Yahoo Finance, The Loadstar, Journal of Commerce (2025-2026)

name: Loadsmart
summary: AI-powered digital freight brokerage and transportation management platform that underwent significant restructuring and layoffs starting Q4 2022 as freight demand cooled sharply, part of an industry-wide FreightTech contraction where VC investment in supply chain tech dropped over 77% year-over-year.
lesson: AI-driven freight-matching algorithms cannot outrun a macro freight-rate collapse — Loadsmart's repeated restructuring mirrored an entire cohort of digital freight brokerages forced to cut deeply in 2022-2023.

name: Uber Freight
summary: AI-driven digital freight brokerage and logistics division of Uber Technologies. Cut roughly 150 brokerage jobs in January 2023 and another 50 in July 2023 amid the broader freight recession that also killed rival Convoy; freight revenue and adjusted EBITDA both declined further in 2024 versus 2023.
lesson: Even with a deep-pocketed parent (Uber) able to subsidize losses, algorithmic freight brokerage could not escape the 2022-2024 freight-rate downturn that forced repeated headcount cuts across the sector.
source: FreightWaves, Yahoo Finance, Uber SEC filings (2023-2024)

name: Forto (FreightHub)
summary: Berlin digital freight forwarder (rebranded from FreightHub) that reached a $2.1B valuation on a $250M Series D in 2022, then endured repeated rounds of layoffs in 2022 and 2023 (roughly 10%, then about one-third of staff — 200 people) as freight rates collapsed, ultimately exploring a sale or merger.
lesson: Peak-cycle unicorn valuations for digital freight forwarders proved unsustainable once shipping rates normalized — Forto's cascading layoffs and exploration of a sale show how quickly a $2B+ valuation can become a survival negotiation.
source: The Loadstar, trans.info, Munich Eye, PitchBook (2022-2024)

name: Vecna Robotics
summary: Massachusetts-based warehouse AMR company replacing forklifts with autonomous robots, which raised a $65M round in 2022 but has undergone recurring rounds of layoffs roughly every two years according to employee reviews, including 40+ staff cut in one round, alongside a CEO change in 2024.
lesson: A cyclical pattern of layoffs every couple of years — even between funding rounds — signals a warehouse robotics company has not found a stable, capital-efficient growth rate matching its cost base.

name: o9 Solutions
summary: Dallas-based AI-powered integrated business planning platform for demand forecasting, supply planning and scenario modeling, bootstrapped for over a decade before taking KKR investment in 2020. Reached a $3.7B valuation in 2023 and serves enterprises including Google, Nike and PepsiCo.
lesson: A decade of bootstrapped, profitable growth before taking outside capital gave o9 negotiating leverage and product-market discipline that many venture-fueled competitors lacked.

name: Blue Yonder
summary: AI-driven supply chain platform (rebranded from JDA Software) covering demand forecasting, inventory optimization and warehouse/transportation management, acquired by Panasonic in 2021. Suffered a major ransomware attack in November 2024 that disrupted grocery and retail customers, but remains a dominant enterprise supply-chain AI vendor.
lesson: Scale and enterprise entrenchment provide resilience — Blue Yonder survived a severe ransomware incident without losing its core market position, though it exposed real cybersecurity risk in concentrated supply-chain SaaS.

name: Kinaxis
summary: Publicly traded Canadian supply chain planning software company whose Maestro (formerly RapidResponse) platform offers concurrent AI-driven planning across demand, supply, inventory and capacity, generating roughly $400M in annual revenue with 300+ enterprise customers including aerospace and life sciences leaders.
lesson: A long product history focused on one core concurrent-planning architecture, continuously layered with AI features (like its Jiver assistant), sustained durable enterprise revenue growth as a public company.
source: Kinaxis investor materials, Supply Chain Digital, SmarterWay.AI (2024-2026)

name: project44
summary: Chicago-based supply chain visibility platform tracking over 1 billion shipments annually across ocean, rail, truckload and last-mile modes, valued at $2.7B in 2023. Acquired AI-forecasting company ClearMetal in 2021 to strengthen predictive visibility capabilities.
lesson: Aggregating carrier and shipment data across every transportation mode creates network effects that make a visibility platform more valuable (and defensible) as it scales, supporting a premium valuation through a SaaS downturn.

name: LLamasoft
summary: Ann Arbor-based leader in AI-powered supply chain design and network planning software, used by Boeing, Nestle and Home Depot. Acquired by Coupa Software in November 2020 for approximately $1.5 billion, becoming Coupa's Supply Chain Design & Planning product line.
lesson: Decades spent building deep, defensible network-design optimization IP for Fortune 500 supply chains translated into one of the largest acquisitions in supply-chain software history.
source: Coupa newsroom, PR Newswire, Supply Chain 24/7 (2020)

name: Fero Labs
summary: New York-based 'white-box' AI company optimizing industrial manufacturing processes (steel, cement, chemicals) for efficiency and emissions reduction, closing a $15M growth round in 2023 led by Climate Investment. Customers including Covestro and Gerdau have identified over $20M in savings.
lesson: Explainable ('white-box') AI that plant operators without data-science backgrounds can trust and act on directly overcame a major adoption barrier in heavy industrial process optimization.

name: Nauto
summary: AI-powered driver and fleet safety platform using in-cab cameras and computer vision to detect distracted driving and prevent collisions, backed by SoftBank Vision Fund, GM, BMW and Allianz. Merged with Nexar in 2026 to form a combined 'physical AI' company focused on real-world driving systems.
lesson: When growth plateaus, a merger of equals with a complementary AI-driving-data company can consolidate scale and data assets rather than each company fighting for share alone.

name: Verusen
summary: Atlanta-based AI platform that harmonizes fragmented materials master data (MRO/spare parts) across ERP and procurement systems using NLP and machine learning, closing a $25M Series B in 2022 led by Scale Venture Partners.
lesson: Solving a narrow, unglamorous but high-value problem — data quality for industrial spare-parts inventory — allowed Verusen to grow with a modest funding footprint relative to flashier supply-chain AI peers.

name: Altana AI
summary: New York AI platform that maps and analyzes global supply chains for trade compliance, risk management and procurement, reaching unicorn status with a $1B valuation after a $200M Series C in 2024, backed by GV, Salesforce Ventures and Generation Investment Management.
lesson: Rising geopolitical trade tensions and tariff volatility created strong tailwind demand for AI-driven supply chain transparency, propelling Altana to unicorn status quickly.

name: Interos
summary: Arlington, Virginia-based AI supply chain risk management platform that synthesizes cyber, financial, ESG and geopolitical risk signals for enterprise and government customers, reaching unicorn status ($1B valuation) in 2021.
lesson: A long incubation period (16 years) focused on building trusted risk-scoring data before AI-native competitors emerged let Interos claim unicorn status once enterprise demand for supply chain risk visibility spiked.

name: RELEX Solutions
summary: Helsinki-based unified retail and supply chain planning platform (demand forecasting, inventory, workforce optimization) that reached a $5.7B valuation on a €500M round led by Blackstone in 2022, driven by retailer demand for resilience amid COVID-era supply disruptions.
lesson: Pandemic-driven supply chain disruption created urgent retailer demand for AI-based planning resilience, and RELEX's unified platform approach let it capture outsized late-stage private equity investment.

name: Antuit.ai
summary: AI-powered demand forecasting and merchandising SaaS platform for retail and CPG companies, majority-owned by a Goldman Sachs Asset Management consortium, acquired by Zebra Technologies in 2021 to extend Zebra's warehouse and forecasting AI portfolio.
lesson: Forecasting-accuracy AI point solutions with strong retail/CPG logos became attractive tuck-in acquisitions for hardware vendors like Zebra seeking to add software-driven recurring revenue.

name: Terra Technology
summary: Demand-sensing and multi-echelon inventory optimization software provider serving P&G, Unilever, Kimberly-Clark and other CPG majors, acquired by E2open in 2016 to combine its demand-signal algorithms with E2open's supply chain network.
lesson: Fourteen years of algorithmic refinement on real-time POS demand signals for blue-chip CPG customers made Terra a natural, de-risked acquisition target for a larger supply chain network platform.

name: Samsara
summary: IoT and AI-powered fleet management platform providing vehicle telematics, dashcam safety AI and supply chain visibility, IPO'd on NYSE in December 2021 at an $11.5B valuation and surpassed $1B in annual recurring revenue by December 2023.
lesson: Combining hardware sensors with AI-driven safety and operations software created high switching costs and recurring revenue that carried Samsara profitably through to a successful public offering.

name: Motive (KeepTruckin)
summary: AI-driven fleet management platform (rebranded from KeepTruckin in 2022) combining ELD compliance, dashcams and spend management, reaching a $2.85B valuation on a $150M raise in 2022 and filing for a Nasdaq IPO under ticker 'MTVE' in late 2025.
lesson: Expanding from a single compliance product (electronic logging devices) into a full AI-powered fleet operations suite let Motive scale toward an IPO in direct competition with Samsara.
source: Built In San Francisco, FreightWaves, Fintool (2022-2025)

name: Symbotic
funding: Public company (Nasdaq: SYM); SPAC valued at $5.5B
summary: AI-powered warehouse automation company deploying robotics and software across all 42 of Walmart's U.S. regional distribution centers. Went public via SoftBank SPAC in 2022 and completed a $520M acquisition of Walmart's own Advanced Systems and Robotics business in January 2025, deepening the partnership.
lesson: Landing and deepening a single anchor enterprise relationship (Walmart) over nearly a decade — rather than diversifying too early — built the scale and trust needed for a landmark reciprocal acquisition deal.

name: Locus Robotics
summary: Autonomous mobile robot (AMR) provider for warehouse and e-commerce fulfillment, reaching a near-$2B valuation on a $117M Series F in 2022. Surpassed 6 billion robot-assisted picks by late 2025 and hit $180M in annual recurring revenue by mid-2026.
lesson: A collaborative, human-in-the-loop robot design (rather than full replacement) eased warehouse adoption friction and let Locus scale ARR faster than fully autonomous or hardware-heavy competitors.
source: PR Newswire, TechCrunch, Built In Boston (2022-2026)

name: Covariant
summary: AI robotics company (founded as Embodied Intelligence) building foundation-model-powered picking, kitting and depalletizing robots for warehouses, raising a $75M Series C led by Index Ventures. Launched RFM-1, a robotics foundation model, in 2024.
lesson: Applying large-scale foundation-model techniques (trained on real warehouse video/action data) to robotic picking gave Covariant a generalization advantage over narrowly hand-engineered picking systems.
source: Wikipedia, Tech Funding News, IEEE Spectrum (2020-2024)

name: Kodiak Robotics
summary: Autonomous trucking company (rebranded Kodiak AI) that went public via SPAC merger with Ares Acquisition Corporation II in 2025 at a $2.5B valuation, having already delivered its first driverless trucks commercially to energy customer Atlas Energy Solutions in the Permian Basin.
lesson: Focusing early on a defined, lower-complexity commercial use case (autonomous trucks for oilfield logistics in Texas) before broader highway deployment let Kodiak reach real revenue ahead of many autonomous trucking peers.

name: Gatik
summary: Autonomous middle-mile trucking company focused on short, repeatable B2B delivery routes for retailers and grocers (Loblaws, Kroger), valued at over $800M. Backed by Koch Industries, Isuzu and Nippon Express, with more than $600M in contracted revenue.
lesson: Narrowing autonomy scope to fixed, repeatable middle-mile routes (rather than open highway long-haul) reduced technical risk enough to reach real contracted commercial revenue years before broader-scope competitors.
source: Gatik press releases, DC Velocity, Forbes (2024-2026)

name: Waabi
summary: Toronto-based autonomous trucking AI company founded by ex-Uber ATG lead Raquel Urtasun, which raised a $750M Series C plus $250M in milestone-based capital from Uber in 2026 to expand into robotaxis — the largest funding round in Canadian startup history.
lesson: A simulation-first, data-efficient AI training approach let Waabi attract massive strategic capital from Uber, betting the same technology stack could generalize from trucking into robotaxis.

name: Aurora Innovation
summary: Self-driving trucking and ride-hailing technology company that went public via SPAC merger in November 2021 at an $11B valuation — the largest AV company IPO at the time — and launched its Aurora Horizon autonomous trucking service on Texas highways.
lesson: Despite a stock price that fell well below its debut for years, Aurora sustained its long-haul autonomous trucking commercialization plan (unlike Embark or TuSimple), showing patient scaling can survive where peers collapsed.

name: AutoStore
summary: Norwegian cube-based automated storage and retrieval robotics company that IPO'd on the Oslo Stock Exchange in 2021 at a $12.4B valuation. Engaged in a multi-year patent dispute with Ocado, ultimately paying Ocado £200M ($257M) to settle in 2023, but continued operating as a profitable public company.
lesson: A costly patent settlement with a direct competitor did not derail AutoStore's underlying business — its cube-storage technology and customer base remained strong enough to absorb a nine-figure litigation cost.

name: Exotec
summary: French warehouse robotics company behind the Skypod goods-to-person system, becoming France's first industrial unicorn with a $2B valuation after a $335M Series D led by Goldman Sachs in 2022. Deployed at Uniqlo, Decathlon, Carrefour and Gap.
lesson: A vertically-climbing robot design that dramatically increases warehouse storage density gave Exotec a clear, quantifiable ROI pitch that fueled rapid European and global retail adoption.
source: Exotec newsroom, Built In, Silicon Canals (2022)

name: Kindred AI
summary: Pioneer in using deep reinforcement learning for AI-powered piece-picking robots in warehouses, acquired by Ocado Group in November 2020 for $262M as part of a $287M combined deal alongside robotic-arm maker Haddington Dynamics.
lesson: Being an early mover in reinforcement-learning-based robotic grasping made Kindred a prized acquisition target for Ocado's push to build a comprehensive grocery-fulfillment robotics platform.
source: Ocado Group newsroom, The Spoon, The Information (2020)

name: Fetch Robotics
summary: Autonomous mobile robot (AMR) provider for manufacturing, distribution and fulfillment workflows, acquired by Zebra Technologies in August 2021 for $290M cash plus Zebra's existing minority stake, valuing the deal at roughly $301M total.
lesson: A modular, software-configurable AMR fleet architecture made Fetch an attractive tuck-in for a hardware incumbent (Zebra) seeking to add mobile robotics to its enterprise asset-tracking portfolio.
source: The Robot Report, BusinessWire, IEEE Spectrum (2021)

name: Optoro
summary: Reverse logistics and returns-optimization technology provider using AI to route, resell or liquidate returned retail inventory for customers like Gap. Shut down its own direct-to-consumer resale site BLINQ in 2021 but continued core operations, ultimately being acquired by Blue Yonder in August 2025.
lesson: Shedding a non-core consumer resale experiment (BLINQ) to refocus on the core enterprise reverse-logistics SaaS product preserved enough value to reach a strategic acquisition years later.

name: Stord
summary: Atlanta-based AI-powered fulfillment and warehouse network platform for independent e-commerce brands, reaching a $3B valuation on a $250M Series F round, having roughly doubled its valuation in under a year. Operates nearly 100 warehouses and processes $15B+ in annual gross merchandise value.
lesson: Building a shared, multi-tenant warehouse network with AI-driven inventory optimization let independent brands access Amazon-like fulfillment speed without owning warehouses, driving rapid re-acceleration in valuation.

name: Blume Global
summary: Multimodal supply chain orchestration and visibility platform used across logistics-heavy workflows, acquired by Australian logistics software giant WiseTech Global for $414M in April 2023 to expand its global freight execution capabilities.
lesson: A long-established multimodal visibility platform with deep logistics-workflow integrations remained a valuable acquisition target for a larger global freight software consolidator even decades after founding.
